sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 15, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 14 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
DOWN didn't work (no impact). The player is still at the same position. Looking at the ascii:
- Row 13 is all black (BBBBB...), which is a wall
- Rows 14-18 have white spaces (WWWWW) at cols 20-24
But the player can't move DOWN because of the wall at row 13.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. The player can only move LEFT/RIGHT along the top corridor. SPACE resets to the start. The red path unfolds when the player reaches it. The target is at the bottom-right.
Maybe the game is not about the player physically moving through the maze. Maybe it's about the player "activating" or "triggering" something by reaching certain positions. The red path unfolding when the player reaches it is a clue.
Let me think about this differently. The red path seems to be a trail that connects the top-right to the bottom-left. When the player reaches the red path, it unfolds. Maybe the player needs to follow the red path by teleporting (using SPACE) to different positions along the path.
Wait, let me re-examine the animation from when the player first reached the red path. The animation showed two objects: the player and the red path. The red path changed shape when the player reached it. This suggests that the red path is dynamic and responds to the player's position.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to look at the red path's boundary more carefully and understand its structure. The red path boundary shows:
[[9, 39], [9, 41], [11, 41], [12, 40], [40, 40], [40, 19], [41, 18], [42, 18]]
This is an L-shaped path:
- Vertical segment: from row 9 to row 40, at col 40
- Horizontal segment: from col 40 to col 19, at row 40
- Then a small extension to row 42, col 18
The target is at rows 49-55, cols 43-49. This is below and to the right of the red path's end.
Hmm, maybe the game is about the player following the red path, and the red path needs to be "extended" to reach the target. When the player reaches the end of the red path, it extends further.
But the player can't move DOWN. Let me try a completely different approach: maybe the player needs to move along the top corridor to a specific position, and then SPACE teleports the player to a different part of the maze.
Let me try moving the player to the far right (where the red path starts) and then pressing SPACE to see if it teleports to a different location.
Actually, I already tried this earlier. When the player was at cols 32-36 and pressed SPACE, it teleported back to cols 14-18 (the start). So SPACE always teleports to the start.
Let me think about this more carefully. The player can move LEFT/RIGHT. SPACE resets to start. DOWN doesn't work. The red path unfolds when the player reaches it. The target is at the bottom-right.
Wait, maybe I need to look at the maze from a different perspective. The white spaces might represent "rooms" or "areas" that the player can visit. The player starts in the top room (rows 8-12). There are white spaces below at rows 14-18, cols 20-24 and 32-38. Maybe the player needs to "enter" these rooms by reaching a specific position and pressing SPACE.
Let me try pressing SPACE when the player is at cols 20-24 (above the white space at rows 14-18).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player is at cols 20-24. Let me check current position and try SPACE
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24:
        print(f"Player: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Try SPACE - maybe it teleports to the white space below
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
